NCRB does not publish a separate snatching head for Karnataka (theft is split only into vehicle/other; snatching is booked under theft 379 or robbery 392). 153 = chain-snatching cases registered by Bengaluru City police in 2023 (city police data via Business Standard). Use as city-level calibration only; state figure not published.
2023. Includes many street/chain snatchings booked as robbery.
2023. Very rare — keep near-zero share.
2023. Night burglaries 4,858 vs day 1,177 (80.5% at night) — NCRB publishes this split directly.
2023. Whole forgery-cheating-fraud group = 7,556 (5.08% of IPC); much online fraud is instead booked under IT Act.
Karnataka was No.1 in India: 8,136 (2021), 12,556 (2022), 21,889 (2023) = ~10.2% of ALL Karnataka cognizable crime. 21,870 of these are IT Act (SLL) cases (33.4% of SLL). NCRB Table 9A.1.
2023. Biggest sub-motives: land/property dispute (1,203) and 'other rioting' (2,197); communal only 18.
2023 road-accident negligence cases (hit-and-run 1,170); all 304A = 12,016 (8.08% of IPC). Related: rash driving (Sec.279 etc.) = 31,908 cases.
2023, = 10.3% of SLL. Split: possession for personal consumption 5,254 vs trafficking 1,509. Bengaluru alone 3,443.
2023, = 21.0% of Karnataka SLL cases (6.4% of all cognizable) — Karnataka books unusually many Gambling Act (Karnataka Police Act matka/betting) cases, mostly outside Bengaluru (city only 639).
2023, = 14.2% of SLL. Karnataka Excise Act; state Prohibition Act cases = 0 (no prohibition in Karnataka).

bengaluruShare.offence: Cyber crimes (17,631 of 21,889) - Bengaluru dominates; also ~52% of ALL-India metro cyber crime
Theft total (10,438 of 24,966; 2nd highest among Indian metros after Delhi)
Gambling Act (639 of 13,768) - gambling is mostly non-Bengaluru

seasonality: Festival-season burglary risk in locked houses is a documented police concern: Bengaluru police run a formal 'locked house' monitoring/patrol scheme for residents travelling during holidays (Deccan Herald 2025), and South-Indian police forces issue Dasara/Deepavali advisories about home burglaries when families travel (e.g., Hyderabad police Dasara advisory, Siasat 2024). Calibrate a burglary uptick in Oct-Nov (Dasara-Deepavali) and school-holiday travel months (Apr-May).

Chain-snatching clusters around festival season when women wear gold in public: Bengaluru saw a same-night series of armed chain-snatchings (Girinagar, Indiranagar, Kottanur, Konanakunte) during Ganesh festival celebrations, Sept 2024 (Hans India). A modest Aug-Nov snatching uplift is defensible.

Monsoon: peer-reviewed India evidence (Blakeslee & Fishman, 'Weather Shocks, Agriculture, and Crime: Evidence from India') finds property crime rises after RAINFALL DEFICITS (drought-hit agricultural incomes), not during monsoon months themselves; direct month-of-monsoon effects on crime are statistically weak. Recommend: no strong monsoon (Jun-Sep) dampening/spike, but allow drought-year property-crime elevation in rural districts. Mark as approximate - Karnataka-specific monthly crime series is not published by NCRB.
NCRB publishes no month-wise data; any monthly curve for the synthetic DB should be gentle (roughly +/-10-15%) with the festival-quarter property-crime bump as the main feature.
timeOfDay: Burglary is overwhelmingly nocturnal - this is hard NCRB data, not anecdote: Karnataka 2023 house-breaking split is 4,858 night vs 1,177 day (80.5% night; NCRB CII-2023 Table 1A.4); Bengaluru City: 878 night vs 264 day (76.9% night; Table 1B.4).

Chain/gold snatching targets morning walkers in the ~5-7 am window: Bengaluru gang case-study of snatchers scouting during 5 am walks (Deccan Herald, Annapoorneshwari Nagar cases); similar morning-walker targeting documented in Cyberabad arrests. Secondary peak in evening/late-night (documented ~10:30 pm festival-night snatchings, Hans India). Calibrate snatching bimodally: early morning + evening/night.
Road-negligence deaths (304A) and rash-driving cases skew to night/early-morning highway hours per road-safety literature, but NCRB gives no hour split - mark as approximation.
Cyber fraud has no time-of-day constraint; complaints are filed next day, so registration timestamps cluster in office hours - safe to model FIR times as daytime.
sources: NCRB, Crime in India 2023 Vol I (Tables 1A.1-1A.5 state-wise IPC/SLL and crime-head tables; 1B.1-1B.5 Bengaluru city tables; 2A.1 murder; 2C.1 kidnapping; 3A.1/3A.6 crimes against women) - https://www.ncrb.gov.in/uploads/files/1CrimeinIndia2023PartI.pdf (mirror: https://data.opencity.in/dataset/crime-in-india-2023)

NCRB, Crime in India 2023 Vol II (Table 9A.1 cyber crimes state-wise 2021-2023; 9A.5 cyber police disposal; 9B.1 Bengaluru cyber 2021-2023) - https://www.ncrb.gov.in/uploads/files/2CrimeinIndia2023PartII.pdf

NCRB, Crime in India 2023 Vol III (Table 17A.1 police disposal of IPC cases: chargesheeting rates by crime head - body 81.5%, property 35.8%, theft 29.8%, burglary 43.6%) - https://www.ncrb.gov.in/uploads/files/3CrimeinIndia2023PartIII.pdf

NCRB Crime in India 2023 release summaries (Karnataka tops cyber crime with 21,889 cases) - https://visionias.in/current-affairs/news-today/2025-09-30/social-issues/national-crime-records-bureau-ncrb-releases-crime-in-india-2023 and https://www.nextias.com/ca/current-affairs/30-09-2025/ncrb-crime-in-india-2023

Business Standard / Vartha Bharati (Jan 2024): Bengaluru City police 2023 data - 153 chain-snatching cases - https://www.business-standard.com/india-news/crimes-against-women-children-rise-in-bengaluru-in-2023-show-police-data-124010400593_1.html

Deccan Herald: Bengaluru police locked-home patrol initiative for residents leaving town - https://www.deccanherald.com/india/karnataka/bengaluru/police-in-south-bengaluru-launch-new-security-initiative-to-protect-locked-homes-3384279

Deccan Herald: Bengaluru chain-snatching gang targeting 5 am morning walkers - https://www.deccanherald.com/india/karnataka/bengaluru/bengaluru-crime-mother-forces-minor-son-into-chain-snatching-arrested-2968229

Hans India (Sept 2024): festival-night armed chain-snatching series in Bengaluru (Girinagar etc.) - https://www.thehansindia.com/karnataka/chain-snatchers-attack-two-women-in-bengaluru-with-sword-loot-gold-jewellery-1006599

Siasat (2024): Hyderabad police Dasara-vacation burglary advisory (festival-season locked-house burglary pattern in South India) - https://www.siasat.com/ahead-of-dasara-vacation-hyderabad-police-issue-advisory-3105636/

Blakeslee & Fishman, 'Weather Shocks, Agriculture, and Crime: Evidence from India' (property crime rises with rainfall deficits, not monsoon months) - https://www.researchgate.net/publication/316332534_Weather_Shocks_Agriculture_and_Crime_Evidence_from_India

NOTES: (1) All Karnataka offence counts are NCRB 2023 (latest available; released Sept 2025); 2024+ data will use BNS sections, not IPC. (2) NCRB has no state-level 'snatching' head - snatching hides inside theft/robbery. (3) Karnataka property-vs-body chargesheeting split is not published; all-India crime-head rates supplied instead, with Karnataka ~4 pts above national overall. (4) Monthly/seasonal and hour-of-day distributions are NOT in NCRB except the day/night burglary split; treat seasonality entries as directional, sourced from news/studies.
